actions.reasoning: The id column is an integer row identifier with no relationship to claim severity. Including it as a feature introduces spurious memorization patterns; dropping it before modeling is standard practice. Most top notebooks either drop it explicitly or select feature columns excluding id.
actions.context_notes: Used in ~8 of 30 notebooks explicitly. Others implicitly drop id by selecting only cat* and cont* columns for the feature matrix. Code patterns: train.drop('id', axis=1) or X=train.drop(columns=['id','loss']).

actions.reasoning: Fitting LabelEncoder separately on train data only assigns integer codes based only on category levels seen in train. Any category level appearing exclusively in test (e.g. a rare multi-level cat) will raise a ValueError or receive an unseen-label error at transform time, breaking the pipeline entirely.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical label-encode action by restricting fit_scope from full_data to train_only.

actions.reasoning: Buckets every category level below 5 percent frequency across all cat1-cat116 columns. This is too aggressive for Allstate because many useful multi-level categorical signals are rare but predictive.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the full categorical encoding path by collapsing many legitimate category levels before encoding.

actions.reasoning: Drops all 116 categorical columns without encoding them first. Feature importance analysis shows that several cat* columns (cat80, cat79, cat87, cat57, cat101) are among the top predictors of claim severity. Discarding all 116 categorical features eliminates the majority of the signal available in the dataset.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical label-encode action by replacing encoding with a destructive drop.

actions.reasoning: All 116 categorical columns hold string values that must be converted to integers for XGBoost, LightGBM, and other tree-based models. LabelEncoder fit on the union of train+test values ensures consistent integer codes across both splits and avoids KeyError for any category level that appears only in test. Used in 14 of 30 collected notebooks. Alternative (OR) to one-hot encoding CA-000014.
actions.context_notes: Fit on combined list of train and test values to handle all category levels. Applied to all object-dtype columns. Alternative (OR) to one-hot encoding CA-000014. Code pattern: for col in cat_cols: le = LabelEncoder(); le.fit(pd.concat([train[col], test[col]])); train[col]=le.transform(train[col]).
evidence_snippets: le_cat_encoder.fit(np.concatenate([train_df[x] for x in cat_cols] + [test_df[x] for x in cat_cols]))

actions.reasoning: Forces the multi-level cat73-cat116 block into an A/B/other mapping. Those columns are specifically the higher-cardinality categorical block, so this collapses most of their information into one catch-all value.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the categorical encoding path by replacing level-preserving encoding with an over-broad manual category map.

actions.reasoning: Clips the training target at the 90th percentile before model fitting. Allstate loss is heavily right-tailed, and the large-claim tail is exactly what drives MAE errors, so clipping target values destroys the signal needed to predict high-loss claims.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the target-transform decision boundary by replacing a reversible log transform with destructive tail clipping on the target.

actions.reasoning: A very low-fold, unsmoothed target encoding on high-cardinality categoricals is a high-variance near-miss. It can memorize fold noise and is not part of the conservative Allstate preprocessing target.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the categorical encoding decision boundary as a model-specific encoding branch with deliberately weak leakage/variance controls.

actions.reasoning: The target loss is heavily right-skewed (mean ~3,037, max ~121,012). Log-transforming it before training causes gradient-boosting models to minimize MAE on the log scale, which is equivalent to minimizing RMSLE on the original scale and dramatically improves fit on large claims. Predictions must be exponentiated (np.exp) before submission. Used in 15 of 30 collected notebooks. Alternative (OR) to log1p transform CA-000027.
actions.context_notes: Applied as train['loss'] = np.log(train['loss']) before model fitting. Inverse transform: predictions = np.exp(model.predict(X_test)). The most common target transform in top-scoring notebooks. Alternative (OR) to log1p CA-000027 (log1p avoids any risk of log(0) but loss is always positive so np.log is safe here).

canonical_params.expression: from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler; scaler = StandardScaler(); cat_cols = [c for c in train.columns if c.startswith('cat')]; train[cat_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(train[cat_cols]); test[cat_cols] = scaler.transform(test[cat_cols])

actions.reasoning: Applying StandardScaler to label-encoded categorical columns treats arbitrary integer codes as if they were measurements on a ratio scale. The arbitrary ordinal assignment by LabelEncoder has no intrinsic magnitude meaning, so z-scoring the codes creates spurious numeric relationships between category levels and distorts the feature space for tree-based models.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical label-encode action by adding an incorrect StandardScaler step on the encoded categorical columns.

actions.reasoning: Standard-scales both continuous features and label-encoded categorical codes after label encoding. Scaling arbitrary category codes creates fake distances between levels which makes this a poor default tree-model pipeline.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the earlier categorical scaling fault and broadened to a model-specific all-feature scaling near-miss.

actions.reasoning: Log-transforms the 14 continuous feature columns instead of the target loss. The cont* columns are already normalized to approximately [0,1] and have no severe skew requiring log transformation. Applying log1p to features distorts their distributions without benefit, while the actual skew problem—the right-tailed target loss—remains untransformed.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical log-transform-target action by misapplying the transformation to continuous feature columns instead of the target.

actions.reasoning: Uniformly bins all normalized continuous predictors into four buckets. The cont features are already dense numeric signals, and coarse binning throws away ordering and resolution that tree models can use directly.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the continuous-feature decision boundary by replacing preserved numeric features with a lossy bucketized representation.

actions.reasoning: One-hot encoding via pd.get_dummies on the combined train+test dataframe is a valid alternative to label encoding: it preserves ordinal independence between category levels and works directly with linear models and neural networks. Used in 10 of 30 notebooks via pd.get_dummies and 6 via OneHotEncoder. Alternative (OR) to label encoding CA-000005.
actions.context_notes: Applied via pd.get_dummies on the combined train+test before splitting, or via sklearn Pipeline with OneHotEncoder. For high-cardinality cat columns this can produce 1,000+ columns and risk memory issues on large machines. Alternative (OR) to label encoding CA-000005.

actions.reasoning: One-hot encodes the id column, which is a unique integer row identifier with 188,318 unique values in train and 125,546 in test. This produces over 188,000 binary columns that are pure identifier noise with no predictive signal for claim severity, catastrophically expanding the feature matrix.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical one-hot encode action by applying it to the wrong column (id) instead of the categorical feature columns.

actions.reasoning: Keeps only 20 predictors from the 130-feature matrix based on one model-importance pass. This is an unstable and overly narrow selection for a competition where weak categorical signals combine across many columns.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the broad feature-preservation path by adding an aggressive post-hoc pruning step.

actions.reasoning: Drops all 14 continuous feature columns. Correlation analysis shows cont2, cont7, cont12, cont11 have the highest correlation with loss among all features. Discarding all 14 continuous features eliminates the most directly predictive numeric signal in the dataset.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical drop-id action by expanding the drop to include all 14 continuous features instead of only the identifier.

actions.reasoning: Multiplies two label-encoded categorical columns. The integer labels are arbitrary, so their product is a spurious numeric interaction rather than a meaningful feature.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the label-encoding branch by treating arbitrary category codes as numeric quantities for interaction features.

actions.reasoning: Applies log1p twice to the target loss, double-compressing the distribution far beyond what is needed. The inverse transform at prediction time would require applying expm1 twice (expm1(expm1(pred))), which inflates prediction errors for large loss values and is rarely correct. A single log or log1p transform is sufficient.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the canonical log1p-transform action by applying it a second time to an already-transformed target.

actions.reasoning: Maps every categorical feature to a binary A-versus-not-A indicator. That may be tolerable for a few binary columns, but it collapses nearly all information in the multi-level categorical block and discards the distinctions preserved by label or one-hot encoding.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the full categorical encoding good action by replacing level-preserving encoding with a coarse A-versus-other mapping across all categorical columns.

actions.reasoning: Buckets levels with fewer than 1,000 rows in the high-cardinality categorical block. This preserves fewer levels than the label/one-hot paths and can erase exactly the rare risk categories that help severity prediction.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from full categorical encoding by adding an over-aggressive rare-level collapse to the high-cardinality columns.

actions.reasoning: Target-encodes all categorical predictors with no smoothing. Even with folds, this makes a high-variance model-specific branch that can chase noisy category means instead of the simpler stable encodings used in the primary spine.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from categorical encoding by swapping in a broad unsmoothed target-encoding path.

actions.reasoning: Min-max scaling the already normalized cont1-cont14 columns on the training split is an unnecessary model-specific detour and uses test feature distribution information for no clear benefit.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the continuous-feature branch by adding unnecessary full-data scaling to features that are already normalized.

actions.reasoning: Uniformly bins label-encoded categorical codes as if adjacent codes had numeric distance. This creates artificial ordinal structure on arbitrary category labels.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from label encoding by applying numeric binning to arbitrary categorical code values.

actions.reasoning: Winsorizes every continuous predictor at the 5th and 95th percentiles even though these features are already bounded. The clipping removes legitimate variation from dense numeric predictors.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the continuous-feature branch by adding an overly aggressive clipping step.

actions.reasoning: Applies a variance threshold across mixed encoded categoricals and continuous columns. Low variance category indicators can still be predictive for severity, so this is an unsafe generic pruning rule.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the full feature-preservation path by adding an unsupervised pruning rule over mixed feature types.

actions.reasoning: log1p(loss) = log(loss + 1) is a numerically safe alternative to np.log for right-skewed positive targets; since all loss values are positive (min > 0), log1p and np.log are functionally equivalent here. Used in 5 of 30 notebooks. Inverse transform at prediction time uses np.expm1. Alternative (OR) to np.log transform CA-000009.
actions.context_notes: Used interchangeably with np.log since loss > 0. Inverse transform: np.expm1(predictions). Alternative (OR) to np.log CA-000009.

actions.reasoning: Adds an unguarded cont1/cont2 ratio. The continuous features are anonymized, so this hand-built ratio has no domain basis and can introduce unstable extreme values when the denominator is small.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Derived from the continuous-feature branch by adding an arbitrary ratio interaction.
